skill.name: writing-skills
skill.description: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment
skill.summary: Test-driven development methodology applied to AI skill documentation creation and testing
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation skill teaching test-driven development for AI skill creation. All 513 static findings are false positives: markdown code examples flagged as shell commands, documentation URLs flagged as network calls, and normal English text matching security keywords. The render-graphs.js utility safely calls graphviz for diagram rendering with hardcoded arguments.

content.user_title: Create and test AI skills with TDD methodology
content.value_statement: Creating AI skills without testing leads to documentation that agents ignore under pressure. This skill applies test-driven development to skill creation: run failing scenarios first, document the failures, write skills addressing those specific gaps, then verify compliance.
actual_capabilities: Guide test-driven skill creation with RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycles
Provide SKILL.md structure templates with frontmatter requirements
Teach Claude Search Optimization for skill discovery
Include utility script for rendering Graphviz diagrams from skills
limitations: Requires subagent support for pressure testing scenarios
Focuses on documentation skills, not code generation
Assumes familiarity with TDD concepts from superpowers:test-driven-development
Diagram rendering requires graphviz (dot) installed on system

use_cases.title: Skill authors creating new documentation
use_cases.description: Follow the RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle to create bulletproof skills that agents comply with under pressure

use_cases.title: Teams standardizing agent behavior
use_cases.description: Create discipline-enforcing skills with explicit rationalization counters and red flags

use_cases.title: Documentation writers improving clarity
use_cases.description: Apply token efficiency techniques and cross-referencing to create concise, discoverable docs

prompt_templates.title: Beginner: Start skill creation checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to create a new skill for [topic]. Walk me through the RED phase: help me write pressure scenarios to run BEFORE I write the skill.

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate: Analyze baseline failures
prompt_templates.prompt: I ran a pressure scenario without the skill and the agent [describe behavior]. Help me identify the rationalization pattern and write a counter for the skill.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced: Bulletproof against rationalization
prompt_templates.prompt: My skill enforces [discipline]. Review my draft and identify potential loopholes where agents might rationalize non-compliance under pressure.

prompt_templates.title: Expert: Full TDD skill creation session
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through creating a complete skill: 1) Design 3+ combined pressure scenarios 2) Document baseline failures 3) Write minimal skill addressing gaps 4) Identify remaining loopholes 5) Verify compliance

output_examples.input: Agent skipped checking skills under time pressure, saying 'I could fix this faster without reading docs'
output_examples.output: Rationalization identified: Speed bias. Counter to add: Explicit 'STOP' checkpoint requiring skill check before any debugging. Red flag: 'I know this already' = read the skill anyway.

output_examples.input: Skill description: 'Use for async testing when tests are flaky'
output_examples.output: Improved: 'Use when tests have race conditions, timing dependencies, or pass/fail inconsistently across runs.' Added searchable symptoms and technology-agnostic triggers.

best_practices: Write pressure scenarios and document baseline failures BEFORE drafting any skill content
Start descriptions with 'Use when...' listing triggering conditions, not workflow summaries
Close loopholes explicitly by listing forbidden workarounds and rationalization counters
anti_patterns: Writing skill documentation before watching agents fail without it
Summarizing the skill workflow in the description field instead of just triggering conditions
Creating multi-language examples instead of one excellent, runnable example

faq.question: Why must I test before writing the skill?
faq.answer: Without seeing how agents actually fail, you cannot know what the skill needs to teach. Testing first reveals the specific rationalizations and gaps your skill must address.

faq.question: What if my skill is just a reference guide?
faq.answer: Even references need testing. Verify agents can find and correctly apply the information with retrieval and application scenarios.

faq.question: How do I know if my skill description is good?
faq.answer: Test it: Does the description start with 'Use when...' and list triggering conditions? Does it avoid summarizing the workflow? Can another agent find it with relevant search terms?

faq.question: What counts as a 'pressure scenario'?
faq.answer: Scenarios combining multiple pressures: time pressure (deadline), sunk cost (already invested effort), authority pressure (human partner wants speed), and exhaustion (long session).

faq.question: Can I edit a skill without re-testing?
faq.answer: No. Any edit requires re-testing with the same scenarios to verify the change did not introduce new loopholes or break existing compliance.

faq.question: What if I cannot get subagent support for testing?
faq.answer: Use the academic review approach: simulate the pressure scenarios mentally, identify likely rationalizations, and add explicit counters. Then test when subagent becomes available.
